1.synthesis:
The narrative pivots from the national debate about kingship to a domestic scene in Gibeah: Kish, a Benjaminite of standing, has lost his donkeys, and sends his son Saul to find them. Saul is introduced with deliberate care — there is no one among the people of Israel more handsome than he; he was a head taller than any of the people. The physical description signals that Saul will be exactly the kind of king Israel's elders requested: impressive in appearance, commanding in stature, a man who looks like a monarch. But the narrator plants an ironic detail immediately: the man Israel's elders wanted is out searching for lost donkeys, dependent on a servant's suggestion to consult the seer because they are running out of bread.

The servant knows of Samuel — there is a man of God in this city who is held in honor; everything he says comes true. He is the pivot on which this scene turns. Saul, focused entirely on the practical problem of the donkeys, needs the servant's guidance to recognize that the seer might also address a much larger question than livestock. The narrative is structured around Saul's unawareness: he does not know that the Lord has already told Samuel, "About this time tomorrow I will send you a man from the land of Benjamin. Anoint him ruler over my people Israel; he will deliver my people from the hand of the Philistines." Saul is searching for donkeys; God is searching for a king.

John Gill observes the theology of providential small things: the lost donkeys bring Saul precisely to the place and moment where he will meet Samuel and receive his anointing. Matthew Henry draws the pastoral application that God often works his greatest purposes through the most ordinary channels — a practical problem, a servant's advice, a journey that almost turns back. The coincidence of timing is highlighted explicitly: Saul and his servant enter the city gate just as the young women are coming out to draw water and just as Samuel is coming up the road.
1.historical_context: Benjamin was the smallest and most politically marginal of the Israelite tribes, which makes Saul's selection from that tribe consistent with the divine pattern of choosing unlikely instruments. Kish's description as a "man of standing" (gibbor hayil) indicates wealth and social position within the tribe. Seers (ro'im) were prophetic figures who received visionary knowledge about hidden or future matters; the term overlaps with "prophet" (nabi) but emphasizes the visual/revelatory mode of reception. Samuel here functions as a seer in the classic sense. The detail that people bring gifts to seers when consulting them (9:7–8) reflects a widespread ancient Near Eastern practice of consulting diviners with payment. The sacrifice at the high place (9:12) was the typical location for communal worship before the Jerusalem temple centralized sacrifice.
1.literary_structure: The section is structured as a journey with multiple delays and redirections: the donkey-search fails, the servant suggests the seer, the money problem is solved, they enter the city gate. Each delay adds to the sense that ordinary human planning is being worked into a pattern shaped by divine purpose. The girls' information at the gate (9:12–13) provides the final orientation just as Samuel himself appears — the meeting is managed so that it appears perfectly natural while being entirely providential.
1.theological_application: Calvin reflects on how God brings his purposes to fulfillment through chains of apparently mundane causation: lost donkeys, empty provisions, a servant's knowledge, the timing of a sacrifice — none of these individually seems significant, yet their convergence produces the anointing of Israel's first king. This warns against limiting divine providence to dramatic interventions: God is as present in the ordinary logistics of daily life as he is in the supernatural moments. Matthew Henry notes that Saul's unawareness of his appointment — he is searching for donkeys, not destiny — is typical of how God chooses his instruments: the person chosen is often the last to know, and the preparation looks nothing like preparation.
1.christology: Saul, handsome and tall, impresses by appearance — exactly what Israel wanted. His physical impressiveness is part of his tragedy: he is the king Israel desired, not the king God would have chosen unprompted. This contrast sets up the explicit lesson of 1 Sam 16:7 when David is anointed: "The Lord does not look at the things people look at. People look at the outward appearance, but the Lord looks at the heart." Christ, in fulfillment of Isaiah's suffering servant portrait, "had no beauty or majesty to attract us to him, nothing in his appearance that we should desire him" (Isa 53:2). The contrast between Saul's imposing height and David's ruddy youth, and between both of them and the despised Nazarene, teaches that divine election operates on criteria invisible to human assessment.

15.synthesis:
The Lord had revealed to Samuel the day before Saul's arrival: "I will send you a man from Benjamin. Anoint him ruler over my people Israel; he will deliver them from the Philistines. I have looked on my people, for their cry has reached me." When Saul appears at the gate, the Lord says to Samuel: "This is the man I spoke to you about; he will govern my people." Samuel meets Saul on the road, and Saul's question is simple — where is the seer's house? Samuel's answer is extraordinary: "I am the seer. Go up ahead of me to the high place, for today you are to eat with me, and in the morning I will send you on your way and will tell you all that is in your heart. As for the donkeys you lost three days ago, do not worry about them; they have been found. And to whom is all the desire of Israel turned, if not to you and your whole family line?"

Saul's response to the last question is the classic response of the person God is about to use: "But am I not a Benjaminite, from the smallest tribe of Israel, and is not my clan the least of all the clans of the tribe of Benjamin? Why do you say such a thing to me?" John Gill reads this as genuine humility, not false modesty — Saul at this moment actually believes he is unworthy of the role being described. Keil and Delitzsch note that the feast that follows, at which Saul is given the seat of honor and the choice portion that had been reserved for him, is a public enactment of his elevation: the seer's treatment of him before the thirty invited guests signals his coming status without yet naming it. The private conversation on the rooftop at dawn (9:25) and the solitary anointing of chapter 10 will follow, completing the sequence from providential encounter to prophetic commissioning.

Matthew Henry is struck by the divine tenderness in the phrase "I have looked on my people, for their cry has reached me" — language that echoes the Exodus (Exod 3:7), in which God saw Israel's affliction and heard their cry. Even in the process of accommodating their sinful request for a king like the nations, God frames his action as compassionate response to their suffering under the Philistines.
15.historical_context: The formula "I have looked on my people, for their cry has reached me" (9:16) deliberately echoes Exod 3:7 ("I have indeed seen the misery of my people in Egypt. I have heard them crying out..."), framing Saul's appointment in the same salvific category as the Exodus. The portion of meat reserved for Saul (9:23–24) — the thigh and what was attached to it — appears to be the choicest cut, suggesting the feast is both hospitality and prophetic pantomime. High places (bamot) were hilltop worship sites throughout Israel; while later condemned as illegitimate after the Jerusalem temple's establishment, they are presented here as normal loci of legitimate sacrifice. Samuel's presiding role at the high place sacrifice confirms his central religious authority.
15.literary_structure: The section is structured in reverse order to the previous section: where the reader knew what Saul did not (that God had spoken to Samuel), here the reader knows no more than Saul — both are discovering Samuel's extraordinary welcome simultaneously. The reserving of the special portion (9:23–24) functions as visible proof that Samuel knew Saul was coming before Saul knew he was going to meet Samuel. The rooftop conversation that closes the section is deliberately suspended — its content is not disclosed, building anticipation for the anointing of chapter 10.
15.theological_application: Saul's humble self-description — from the smallest tribe, the least clan — echoes the self-descriptions of other chosen figures: Gideon ("my clan is the weakest in Manasseh, and I am the least in my family," Judg 6:15), Moses ("I am slow of speech and tongue," Exod 4:10). Calvin observes that God consistently chooses those who are genuinely humble at the moment of calling — pride grows afterward, in some cases fatally, as Saul's story will demonstrate. The tragedy of Saul is not that he was unfit at the beginning but that the humility of chapter 9 did not persist through the tests of chapters 13 and 15. For those in leadership, Saul's early humility and later pride is a cautionary pattern: the virtues that secure calling can be abandoned in the exercise of power.
15.christology: The divine statement that God has "looked on" his people and their "cry has reached" him, even in the context of appointing a human king, establishes that the entire monarchy of Israel is embedded in the redemptive logic of Exodus compassion. God does not give Israel a king despite caring about them; he gives Israel a king precisely because he cares. The ultimate king — the Son of David — is the fullest expression of this Exodus-shaped compassion. In Christ, God looks on humanity in its affliction and sends not merely a human deliverer but himself: the Word made flesh. The reserved portion that awaits Saul at the feast before he arrives anticipates the eschatological feast prepared by Christ for his people: "I confer on you a kingdom, just as my Father conferred one on me, so that you may eat and drink at my table in my kingdom" (Luke 22:29–30).
